questions.title: Cases We Have in Greek
questions.answerText:
There are five (5) cases in Greek:
Nominative — the subject of the sentence
Genitive — shows possession ('of')
Dative — the indirect object ('to/for')
Accusative — the direct object
Vocative — used for direct address

questions.title: Genders in Greek
questions.answerText:
There are three (3) genders in Greek:
Masculine
Feminine
Neuter

questions.title: Five Things to Look for When Analyzing a Greek Verb
questions.answerText:
Person — 1st, 2nd, or 3rd
Number — Singular or Plural
Tense — Present, Imperfect, Future, Aorist, Perfect, Pluperfect
Voice — Active, Middle, or Passive
Mood — Indicative, Subjunctive, Optative, Imperative, Infinitive, Participle

questions.title: Greek Vowels
questions.answerText:
There are seven (7) vowels in Greek:
α, ε, η, ι, ο, υ, ω
Short vowels: ε, ο (always short); α, ι, υ (can be short)
Long vowels: η, ω (always long); α, ι, υ (can be long)

questions.title: Diphthongs
questions.answerText:
Definition: A diphthong is the combination of two vowels that are pronounced together as a single sound.
Greek Diphthongs:
Diphthong
Sound / Pronunciation
αι
like 'aisle'
ει
like 'eight'
οι
like 'oil'
υι
like 'we'
αυ
like 'now'
ευ
like 'feud'
ου
like 'food'
ηυ
rare; long eu sound

questions.title: Three Kinds of Accents in Greek
questions.answerText:
Acute ( ΄ ) — ὀξεῖα — can appear on any of the last three syllables (e.g., ἄνθρωπος)
Grave ( ` ) — βαρεῖα — replaces an acute on the final syllable when followed by another word (e.g., καλὸς ἄνθρωπος)
Circumflex ( ͂ ) — περισπωμένη — appears only on long syllables; only on the last two syllables (e.g., δοῦλος)

questions.title: Liquid Verbs
questions.answerText:
A. Definition
Liquid verbs are verbs whose stem ends in a liquid consonant: λ, μ, ν, or ρ. They form their future tense by adding -εσ- (which then contracts), and their aorist without the typical σ suffix, instead lengthening the stem vowel.
B. Three Examples: μένω, κρίνω, αἴρω
i. μένω (I remain / I stay)
Present Active Indicative:

questions.title: Deponent Verbs
questions.answerText:
Definition
Deponent verbs are verbs that have Middle or Passive forms but carry Active meanings. They have no active voice forms, yet they are translated actively in English.
Five Examples with Conjugations
1. ἔρχομαι — I come / I go
Indicative (Present Middle/Passive):
Person
Singular
Plural
1st

ἐρχόμεθα

Imperative:
Person
Singular
Plural
2nd
ἔρχου
ἔρχεσθε
